observations.recommendation:
1.4 To further enhance financial accuracy and timeliness, we recommended that
the MA promptly adjust all reconciling items, including FT and stale checks, to
maintain up-to-date and reliable financial records.

2.6 We commended Management for its substantial compliance with regulations
goingverning the granting, utilization, and liquidation of CAs issued to SDOs. To further
enhance financial accuracy, we recommend that the MA conduct a thorough review
of all entries to ensure precise and reliable financial reporting.

3.6 We recommended that Management direct the following:

a. MT to (i) Provide the MA with a duly certified list of taxpayers indicating the
names and corresponding amounts due and collectible for the year, which
serves as the official basis for setting up RPT and SET receivables; and (ii)
ensure the regular updating of the RPT Account Register (RPTAR) to
incorporate revised assessed values with the year of reassessment,
corresponding tax dues, cancellations due to property transfers, updated
property details, names of new owners, and new tax declaration numbers to
maintain the accuracy and completeness of records.; and
b. The MA and the MT to jointly ensure that the ending balances of the RPT and
SET receivable accounts are regularly reconciled with the List of Delinquent
Taxpayers. This will promote the accuracy of financial records, enhance
internal consistency, and support more reliable financial reporting.

4.12 We recommended that Management consider the following remedial
measures:

a. assign personnel to retrieve records and documents supporting the reported
“CIP – Buildings and Other Structures” account under GF and determine the
proper treatment, whether it will qualify for derecognition to establish the
accuracy of the affected account balances; and
b. direct the Property Officer to (i) prioritize the registration of all motor vehicles
owned by the Municipality with the LTO to prevent possible loss and misuse
of the assets and to avoid incurring penalties, fines or sanctions from the LTO.;
(ii) facilitate the insurance of all motor vehicles owned by the Municipality to
ensure the LGU is indemnified or compensated for any damage or loss due to
possible casualties that may happen on these insurable assets; and (iii)
maintain complete detailed Property Cards (PCs) to ensure that property
records are properly reconciled with the accounting records.

5.5 We recommended that Management undertake the following courses of
action:

a. instruct the MA to:
i. draw the following accounting entries to correct the misstatements in
Leave Benefits Payable and Government Equity accounts; and
Particulars Dr. Cr.
Government Equity ₱479,890.85
Leave Benefits Payable ₱479,890.85
ii. reclassify the non-current portion of the Leave Benefits Payable for the
proper presentation of the account in the Statement of Financial Position.
b. direct the Human Resource Management Office to:
i. initiate and prioritize the reconciliation and correction of balances as per
audit in the leave cards; and
ii. revisit its process for maintaining leave cards, such as regularly reconciling
leave cards and implementing other internal control measures to ensure
their accuracy; and
c. set aside additional reserve funds for the accumulated compensated leave
credits as of year-end; henceforth, ensure the adequacy of the funds provided
for the leave credits earned thereafter by including the said benefits in the
annual budget.

6.9 We recommended that the Management instruct the Bids and Awards
Committee (BAC) to:

a. provide a valid explanation for post-qualifying the contractor despite non-
compliance with technical requirements, particularly the submission of proof
of ownership for the pledged equipment or certification of equipment
availability from the lessor/vendor for the project duration;
b. establish complete qualification and experience data in the Bid Data Sheet
(BDS), including the required minimum years of experience for key personnel;
and
c. require the winning contractor to submit their Department of Labor and
Employment (DOLE)-approved Cash, Securities, and other Property (CSHP)
within the required period.

7.6 However, we recommend that the Municipal Mayor instruct the:

a. BAC and Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Office to (i) procure
directly from qualified suppliers with readily available goods and items; (ii)
strictly comply with the shopping procurement process, particularly by canvassing
prices from at least three suppliers or contractors; and (iii) transact only with a
legally registered supplier to ensure transparency in the procurement process; and
b. MA to (i) make the necessary adjusting journal entry to correct the erroneous
recording of the remote-controlled drone.

8.7 We commended the Management for substantial compliance with the full
disclosure, utilization and reporting of LGSF-SBDP of the NTF-ELCAC, particularly
in implementing PPAs aimed at achieving inclusive and sustainable peace. However,
we recommend that Management direct the Bids and Awards Committee to (a) submit a
valid explanation for post-qualifying the contractor despite non-compliance with technical
requirements, specifically the failure to submit proof of ownership for the equipment
pledged or certification of availability of equipment from the lessor/vendor for the
duration of the project; (b) establish complete qualification and experience data,
including the required minimum years of experience for key personnel in the BDS; and
(c) require the winning contractor to submit the DOLE-approved CSHP within the
prescribed period.

9.3 We commended the Management for its compliance with budget
appropriation for PS within the limitation set by R.A. No. 7160 and for maintaining
complete documentary requirements for claims of salaries and wages of casuals and
job orders personnel. However, to further improve personnel management and
strengthen internal controls, we recommend that the Management instruct the
Municipal HRMO to (a) prepare a Statement of Duties and Responsibilities for casual
personnel to evaluate their accomplishment vis-à-vis assigned task/duties and (b)
submit the required locator slips/travel orders and leave applications, if any, to verify
their absence during headcount and ensure proper accountability.

10.4 We recommended that GFPS (i) continue promoting and ensuring gender
mainstreaming in local policymaking, planning, programming, budgeting,
implementation, monitoring, and evaluation, as mandated by PCW, DILG, DBM and
NEDA JMC No. 2013-01; (ii) strive for the full implementation of GAD-related PPAs
within the allocated budget by ensuring its judicious utilization; and (iii) regularly
update the GAD database with reliable gender statistics and sex-disaggregated data
to strengthen planning, budgeting, programming and policy formulation.

15.7 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the MA and persons
responsible/liable to immediately enforce the settlement of audit suspensions,
disallowances and charges pursuant to COA Circular No. 2009-006 dated September
15, 2009.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the MT to (i) ensure that
all collections are promptly remitted by the collectors at the end of the day or no later than
the next remittance day to minimize the possible risk of misappropriation or loss; and (ii)
require all collectors to submit RAAF every end of the month for better monitoring and
observe proper accountability for AF to prevent mishandling of these forms.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct:
a. the MA comply with all the procedures provided in the COA Circular No.
2023-008 dated August 17, 2023 to resolve the dormant accounts;
b. the MA, in coordination with the MT, reviews the recorded receivables
regarding electricity charges at the public market, offices, and commercial
buildings to ensure they contain the necessary details/information. Efforts
should be exerted to collect the accounts through the issuance of demand
letters.

We commended the management for its significant compliance with the
provisions of COA-DBM-DILG-GCC-DND Joint Circular No. 2015-01 relative to the
granting of CA for CF. However, we recommended instructing the Secretary of the
MPOC to ensure that 2/3 of its members sign the Resolution to formalize the
approval. Additionally, we recommended the MA to establish and maintain a SL to
record the details of CAs and the liquidation of the CF.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the:
a. MA to (i) conduct a thorough review of the inventory accounts and tally them
with the concerned offices; (ii) derecognize the issued welfare goods and other
supplies in the books; and (iii) adjust the misclassification of entries for the
fair presentation of the inventory accounts; and
b. MT and other concerned offices to (i) prepare and submit the SSMI, RIS,
and distribution lists to the MAO for adjustments; (ii) conduct complete
inventory taking of all inventories and prepare the corresponding RPCI; and
(iii) reconcile the RPCI with inventory accounts in the SL to reflect the correct
inventory of supplies.

We recommended that Management consider the following remedial
measures:

a. instruct the MA to draw adjusting entries for (i) capitalization of re-
construction of Motorpool Storage Room and construction of additional
niches for municipal cemetery; (ii) recognition of donated motor vehicle;
b. create an Inventory Committee composed of an adequate number of
members, with at least one member each from Accounting and Property
Division Office to conduct a comprehensive inventory taking in accordance
with COA Circular No. 2020-006; and
c. instruct the Property Officer to properly identify all unserviceable, defective
or obsolete PPEs and initiate disposal thereof, subject to proper disposal
procedures to avoid wastage and unnecessary consumption of storage space.
Further, once disposed, the Accounting Unit should derecognize them in the
books so that the fixed assets reflected in the financial statements represents
the actual assets of the Municipality.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the MA
Accountant to (i) perform periodic analysis and validation of the fund transfers and
determine the source entities concerned; (ii) make representations with the identified
SAs to facilitate the proper liquidation and/or return of fund transfers; and (iii)
conduct cleaning of the accounts by following the procedures for the write-off of
dormant accounts, specifically those from unknown source agencies.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct:
a. the MT and MA to (i) remit the shares/income of the Barangay and Province
promptly, providing additional funds for their priority PPAs; and (ii) trace back the
amount of ₱124,970.12 to determine the transaction and settle its creditor/s; and
b. the MA to reclassify the financial assistance to Trust Liabilities – DRRM for
the fair presentation of accounts.

We recommended that Management:
a. compel the contractor to conduct repairs on the damaged road projects to
restore the quality assurance of the project;
b. instruct the Bids and Awards Committee to (a) submit a valid explanation
for post-qualifying the contractor despite non-compliance with technical
requirements, specifically the list of key personnel to be assigned to the
contract to be bid; (b) establish complete qualification and experience data,
including the required minimum years of experience for key personnel in the
Bid Data Sheet (BDS); and (c) require the winning contractor to submit their
DOLE-approved CSHP within the required period; and
c. direct the ME to submit proof of posting of tarpaulin under COA Circular
No. 2013-004 dated January 30, 2013.

However, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the:
a. MA to (i) recognize subsequent cash assistance intended for disaster response
in the TL-DRRMF account and include it in the LDRRMF Utilization
Report; and
b. MSWDO to (i) submit the certified list of affected families to verify eligible
beneficiaries; (ii) reconcile the actual number of relief goods distributed for
proper recording; and (iii) submit the RDS for purchases amounting to
₱300,000.00 evidencing issuances and receipts.

We recommend the Municipal Mayor to instruct the MT and AMT to:
a. submit a proposal to the Sangguniang Bayan to establish and institutionalize
the use of PILAA, ensuring a more realistic basis of business taxes in the
absence of the required audited financial statements, ITRs, or Sworn
Statement of Gross Sales or Receipts;
b. complete the filing of records of taxpayers, including the duly approved
application, assessment, and financial records, to support their computation
of business tax and subject them to audit;
c. regularly examine the books of accounts of business establishments to verify
the accuracy of declared gross sales or receipts used in computing business
taxes, in accordance with Section 171 of RA No. 7160; and
d. collect business taxes from outside contractors who engaged in business
within the Municipality with bunkhouses or other structures that may be
considered as project office, as a basis for imposing business taxes.

We reiterated our previous year’s recommendations that management (a)
immediately transmit to the AT all the unsubmitted accounts; and (b) set up
procedures that will strictly observe the timeframes for the submission of accounts
and financial reports.

We recommended that management:
a. enforce strict waste segregation at the source by (i) implementing an
ordinance with fines and penalties for violators and (ii) conducting a
comprehensive information and education campaign to educate and engage
constituents, especially market vendors, in proper waste segregation
practices; and
b. implement quality control measures to ensure the safety of waste collectors
and other relevant personnel by providing them with personal protective
equipment to mitigate the risks associated with handling solid waste.

We recommended that GFPS continue its efforts in promoting and ensuring
gender mainstreaming in local policymaking, planning, programming, budgeting,
implementation, monitoring, and evaluation, as required by PCW, DILG, DBM and
NEDA JMC No. 2013-01.

We commended the Management and encourage them to continuously
adhere with the revenue regulations on the proper withholding and remittance of
taxes withheld within the prescribed period.

We recommended that Local Chief Executive instruct the MAO to continue
its efforts to ensure that all withheld premium contributions and loan amortizations
are remitted intact.

We recommended that management instruct the Municipal Account to (i)
strictly comply with the provisions of R.A. No. 10606 on the full remittance of
amounts due to PhilHealth; and (b) exert more effort to trace the previous years’
balance of ₱70,906.25 and ensure its remittance to avoid the accumulation of
discrepancies. Further, review postings to the respective SLs and reconcile/adjust any
differences immediately.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the MA and persons
responsible/liable to (i) immediately enforce the settlement of audit suspensions
pursuant to COA Circular No. 2009-006 dated September 15, 2009; and (ii) be more
cautious in the implementation of existing accounting and auditing rules and
regulations in processing of claims to avoid possible audit suspensions and
disallowances.

Municipal Treasurer and concerned Offices to (i) prepare and furnish the Municipal Accounting Office (MAO) on a monthly basis the copies of Summary of Supplies and Materials Issued (SSMI) supported with Requisition Issue Slip (RIS) and/or proof of distribution of goods and items; and (ii) reconcile the RPCI with Inventory accounts in the General Ledger (GL).
b. Municipal Accountant to (i) conduct a thorough review of the Inventory accounts and tally with the concerned Offices; (ii) derecognize in the books the items which were already consumed and/or distributed to end-users in a timely manner; (iii) record all procurement of inventory supplies in the books using the Perpetual Inventory System; and
c. Designated Inspection and Property Officers to ensure that all items are fully delivered and received before accepting and signing the AIR.

observations.recommendation:
1.19 We recommended that Municipal Mayor instruct the:
a. Municipal Treasurer and concerned Offices to (i) prepare and furnish the
MAO on a monthly basis the copies of SSMI supported with RIS and/or proof
of distribution of goods and supplies; and (iii) reconcile the RPCI with
Inventory accounts in GL;
b. Municipal Accountant to (i) conduct a thorough review of the Inventory
accounts and tally with the Offices concerned; (ii) derecognize in the books
the items which were already consumed and/or distributed to end-users in a
timely manner; (iii) record all procurement of inventory supplies in the books
using the Perpetual Inventory System; and
c. Designated Inspection and Property Officers to ensure that all items are fully
delivered and received before accepting and signing the AIR.

2.20 We recommended that Management consider the following remedial
measures; to wit:
a. instruct the Municipal Accountant to (i) draw the adjusting entry for the
derecognition of demolished access bridge at Sta. Fe; and (ii) assign personnel
for the retrieval of records and documents to support retrieval for records
and documents to support the reported “Furniture and Fixtures” account
under the SEF and to determine the proper treatment whether it will qualify
for derecognition to establish the accuracy of the affected account balances;
b. advise the Municipal Treasurer to (i) to conduct a complete and detailed
physical inventory of all local road networks owned by the Municipality and
report the results thereof using the RPCLRN; and (ii) maintain Local Road
Inventory Map in coordination with MPDO and Property Cards for each
type of properties as the basis for reconciliation of PPE accounts with
accounting records; and (iii) cause the proper appraisal of all insurable
properties of the Municipality pursuant to Section 4(c) of R.A. No. 656 and
COA Circular No. 2018-02; and (iv) submit the duly accomplished PIF to
GSIS, copy furnished this Office for the proper evaluation of insurance
coverage of insurable assets and the computation of premiums thereon.

3.13 We recommended that (i) Municipal Accountant (a) thoroughly review the
recorded accounts payable to determine whether the claims are still valid in
coordination with the concerned Offices/payees and cause the immediate reversion
to the unappropriated surplus that has been long outstanding, no actual claim,
without administrative or judicial filed or not covered by perfected contract or
record; and (b) draw an adjusting entry to take up the settled obligations amounting
to ₱146,114.50; and (ii) Head of Offices to ensure all transaction documents subject
to obligation are valid duly signed/approved.

4.15 We commended the MAO and MTO for (i) depositing all collections intact
and regularly (ii) periodic reconciliation of cash on hand and CIB accounts records,
and (ii) preparation and submission of BRS of all CIB accounts regularly. Further,
we appreciate the effort of Management in exercising prudence in the handling and
disbursing of funds. However, we recommend the Municipal Accountant find the
cause of the cash deficit under the TF and ensure that disbursements are within the
entrusted fund to prevent incurring a cash deficit.

5.34 We recommended that Management:
a. compel the contractor to conduct a repair on the damaged road projects to
restore quality assurance of the project;
b. instruct the (i) MAO to withhold retention money or to require the contractor
to issue in favor of the government, irrevocable standby letters of credit from
a commercial bank, bank guarantees, or surety bonds callable on demand
instead of retention money; (ii) BAC to (a) submit a valid explanation in post-
qualifying the contractor despite non-compliance with the document of the
statement of all on-going government and private contracts including contract
awarded but not yet started; (b) compel the contractor to post-warranty security
to cover structural defects after the issuance of final acceptance; and (c) require
the winning contractor to submit their DOLE-approved CSHP with correct
details; and
c. direct the Municipal Engineer to (i) prepare contract plans for infrastructure
projects before the commencement of the bidding process; and (ii) submit
proof of posting of tarpaulin in compliance with COA Circular No. 2013-004
dated January 30, 2013.

6.20 We recommended that MHO instruct the:
a. BHWs concerned to submit a written explanation on the discrepancies
between the submitted DTRs and logbooks of BHWs; provide valid proofs
that they physically report for work and have provided direct health/support
care as well as valid reasons for failure to sign in the logbook of the Barangay;
b. Punong Barangays, being the head of the health facility, to ensure that DTRs
were based on the logbook to avoid discrepancies in the amounts to be
claimed;
c. ensure that reported accomplishments involved COVID-19 response in line
with the prevention, detection, isolation, treatment, rehabilitation and
vaccination strategy.

7.21 On the other hand, we recommend Management instruct the:
a. Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Assessor provide an explanation why
the old TD was used instead of the new TD for January, 2022 payments of
RPT/SET;
b. Municipal Assessor to submit the requested copies of Tax Declaration and
supporting documents to evaluate further the correctness of assessed value; and
c. the Municipal Accountant ensure that the Provincial and Barangays receive
their share of RPT and SET in a timely manner for their additional financial
resources.

8.9 We commended the Management for compliance in appropriating the
budget for PS within the limitation set by RA No. 7160 and exerting its efforts to
maintain complete documentary requirements for claims of salaries and wages of
casuals and job orders personnel. However, we recommend that management instruct
the HRMO to prepare a statement of duties and responsibilities for casual personnel
to evaluate their accomplishment vis-à-vis assigned tasks/duties.

9.19 We appreciated the efforts of Management for substantial implementation of
the planned disaster-related PPAs for the current year. However, we recommended
that the (a) MDRRMC identify the specific implementation schedule of PPAs and
include the unexpended balances of the LDRRMF under the STF in the LDRRMFIP;
and (b) Municipal Accountant (i) transmit the unsubmitted DVs and aforementioned
lacking supporting documents to the Audit Team; and (ii) ensure that only claims
with complete documentations are processed for payment.

10.10 We recommended that Management instruct the Municipal Accountant and
the Municipal Treasurer including all concerned employees to (a) immediately
transmit to the Audit Team all the unsubmitted accounts and financial reports; (b)
set-up procedures that will strictly observe the timeframes on the submission of
accounts and financial reports; and (c) impose appropriate sanctions against those
officials/employees concerned who have continuously disregarded the set deadline
for the submission of the accounts/reports.

11.9 We recommended that GFPS continue its efforts in promoting and ensuring
gender mainstreaming in local policymaking, planning, programming, budgeting,
implementation, monitoring and evaluation as required by PCW, DILG, DBM and
NEDA JMC No. 2013-01.

12.6 We commended the Management for the timely remittances of taxes withheld
within the prescribed period. However, we recommended that the Municipal
Accountant and the staff concerned tally the SL balances with the GL controlling
accounts for the Due to BIR before remittances are made so that all the taxes withheld
during the period will be remitted correctly.

13.7 We commended the Management for regular and full remittances of
premium contributions and loan amortizations withheld in CY 2022. However, we
recommended that the Municipal Accountant and the staff concerned to exert efforts
to trace the undocumented balances of ₱199,185.16 and reconcile/remit the amount
of ₱13,782.33 representing net under remittances.

14.7 We appreciated the effort of Management for full remittance of current
premiums and loan amortizations to Pag-IBIG during the year. However, we
recommend that the Municipal Accountant and the Accounting staff in charge of Pag-
IBIG remittances trace and reconcile the prior years’ balances of ₱60,681.87.

15.6 We commended the Management for regular and full remittances of
premium contributions withheld in CY 2022. However, we recommended that
management instruct the Municipal Accountant to exert more effort to trace back the
previous year’s balance of ₱70,906.25 and cause its remittance to avoid the
accumulation of discrepancies.

16.6 We recommended that Management and the persons responsible/liable
settle immediately the audit suspensions, disallowances and charges pursuant to
COA Circular No. 2009-006 dated September 15, 2009.

We recommended that management direct the: Municipal Accountant to (i) refrain from granting cash advances to accountable officers in amounts beyond their approved maximum cash accountability and those AOs with expired fidelity bonds so as to protect the interest of the Municipality from possible losses in case the former failed to render account for such cash advances or had abandoned their post; (ii) stop the practice of granting new cash advances without the liquidation of previous ones; and (iii) require the concerned personnel to liquidate their cash advances within the reglementary period provided by law; and (iv) the employee in charge of the Confidential Fund to make representation with the Intelligence and Confidential Fund Audit Unit (ICFAU) of COA Central Office for the prompt issuance of credit notice on prior years’ unliquidated cash advances which shall be the basis for recording thereafter.
observations.reason: There were instances when the amount of cash advances granted to Accountable Officers (AOs) exceeded the maximum cash accountability covered by their fidelity bond. On the other hand, one AO was granted cash advances despite having no application for fidelity bond with the Bureau of Treasury. Some AOs were granted additional cash advances despite having outstanding cash advances in their possession.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Accountant: exert diligent effort to trace the source of undocumented and unremitted balance of ₱249,403.62 by comparing the alpha list of employees with processed payrolls and statement of remittances; adhere faithfully to R.A. No. 8291 to avoid incurrence of penalty and interest on delayed or non-remittance of amounts due to GSIS; and instruct the Accounting staff to (i) analyze the causes of discrepancy between the amounts withheld and the remittances; (ii) deduct from the salaries of employees concerned any deficiencies in the premiums withheld and refund thru the voucher system the excess deductions in the payroll; (iii) for excess remittances to the GSIS, send a request for possible offsetting in the succeeding month’s remittances provided the individual employees affected have been identified; and (iv) see to it that all necessary adjustments are taken up in the books thru issuance of a JEV.
observations.reason: The Accounting Unit still failed to trace the source of the undocumented balance.

observations.recommendation:
1.3	We recommended and management agreed to:
instruct the Municipal Accountant to draw the following adjusting journal entries to correct the misstatements in the different accounts affected:
advise the Municipal Accountant to coordinate with the Provincial Accountant and secure Report of Collections on RPT and Taxes on Sand, Gravel and Other Quarry Products of the PGZ every year-end as basis of recognizing the Receivables and Income accruing to the Municipality; (b) keep track of the transfers/remittances made by the PGZ to ensure that the set-up of RPT/SET Receivables and Deferred Charges are duly adjusted upon receipt/remittance of Municipal share from the PGZ; and
instruct the Municipal Treasurer to provide the Municipal Accountant the List of Delinquent Stallholders every end of the year to record/adjust the outstanding Accounts Receivables; and prepare the Real Property Tax Account Register (RPTAR) as basis in setting up the RPT/SET Receivable at the beginning of the year.

2.3       We recommended and management agreed to (a) advise the Municipal Treasurer to conduct periodic physical count of inventories and submit the monthly report on utilization to the Accounting Office for immediate recording of the issued/consumed inventories; and (b) instruct the Municipal Accountant to draw the following adjusting journal entries to correct the misstatement of the Inventories accounts:

3.10     We recommended and management agreed to instruct the Municipal Accountant to draw the following adjusting journal entries to correct the misstatement of the Property, Plant and Equipment and Government Equity accounts:

4.7       We recommended and management agreed to instruct the Municipal Accountant to transfer the completed projects in the CIP-Buildings and Other Structures account to the appropriate Buildings and Other Structures account and apply the accounting procedures for the provision of depreciation;  and draw the following adjusting journal entries to correct the misstatement of the Property, Plant and Equipment and Government Equity accounts:

We recommended and management agreed to instruct the Municipal Accountant to (i) refrain from granting cash advances to an Accountable Officer with no approved application for fidelity bond; (ii) require the personnel concerned to liquidate their cash advances including the unspent balances in their possession within the reglementary period; and (iii) adhere to the guidelines prescribed under COA-DBM-DILG-GCG-DND Joint Circular No. 2015-01 on the granting, recording, and reporting of confidential and/or intelligence funds for CY 2017 and the years thereafter.

advise the Municipal Treasurer to (i) create an Inventory Committee to conduct complete and detailed physical inventory of all properties owned by the Municipality and report the results thereof using the Report on Physical Count on Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE); and (ii) maintain Local Road Inventory Map and Property Cards for each type of properties as basis for reconciliation of PPE accounts with accounting records;
instruct the Municipal Accountant to (i) maintain Property, Plant and Equipment Ledger Cards (PPELC) for each type of properties and Local Road Network Ledger Card (LRNLC) for the local road networks to facilitate tracing of balances and transactions as well as reconciliation with the GL controlling accounts;

7.6        We recommended and management agreed to:
instruct the Bids and Awards Committee, together with its TWG and Secretariat to (i) scrutinize thoroughly the documents submitted by the bidders to ensure their authenticity, veracity, and completeness as basis for the evaluation of the most advantageous and reasonable bids; (ii) conduct efficiently the post-qualification of bidders particularly in determining the sufficiency of contractors’ manpower and equipment capacity on all awarded projects; and (iii) submit copies of the contracts and all supporting documents within five working days from execution to the Audit Team;
direct the Municipal Accountant to coordinate with the BAC for the completion and submission of the documentary requirements noted in audit and ensure that only disbursement vouchers with complete documentation are processed and presented for payment; and
advise the Municipal Engineer to conduct thorough review of the computation of ABC to ensure its compliance with the latest guidelines from the DPWH.

8.7      We recommended and management agreed to require the Municipal Engineer to strengthen the LGU’s policies and procedures in the preparation of the Program of Works (POW) and Approved Budget for the Contract (ABC) and enforce the provision for Construction Safety and Health Program pursuant to DOLE D.O. No. 13 series of 1998 and Annex A of the RIRR of R.A. No. 9184 to promote the occupational safety of workers and road users during the implementation of the infrastructure projects.

9.8	We recommended and management agreed to instruct the (a) Local Development Council to undertake a thorough evaluation of the programs, projects and activities to be funded under the 20% Development Fund and refrain from charging expenditures which do not partake the nature of investment and capital expenditures; and (b) Municipal Engineer and Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator to regularly monitor the status of project implementation to ensure the completion of the seven developmental projects on time.

We recommended and management agreed to advise the LDRRM Officer to (a) include in the LDRRMF Investment Plan the programs, projects and activities to be undertaken out of the unexpended balance of previous years; (b) strive for the full implementation of LDRRM-related PPAs by utilizing judiciously the budget set aside for said purposes; and (c) submit the report on the utilization of DRRMF to the Office of the Auditor on or before the 15th day after the end of each month.

We recommended and management agreed to (i) consider the purchase of additional machineries, equipment and vehicles for waste collection, processing, transport and disposal; (ii) intensify the campaign to stop totally the open burning of wastes, particularly in the rural areas; and (iii) continuously educate the market vendors on the proper waste segregation.

We recommended and management agreed to adopt the following courses of action:
instruct the GFPS through the Municipal Planning and Development Officer to establish GAD database to include gender statistics and sex dis-aggregated data to serve as inputs for planning, budgeting, programming and policy formulation;
strive for the full implementation of GAD-related PPAs by utilizing judiciously the budget set aside for said purposes; and
comply with the provisions of the aforementioned Circular on the submission of GAD Accomplishment Report to facilitate audit of the utilization of GAD budget and the efficiency and effectiveness of interventions in addressing gender issues.

13.6       We recommended that management instruct the Accounting staff in charge of remittance to GSIS to (a) tally the subsidiary ledger (SL) balances with the General Ledger (GL) controlling account for Due to GSIS before remittances are made so that all the premiums and loans withheld during the period will be remitted in full; and (b) perform the necessary reconciliation with the GSIS to trace the undocumented balance of ₱249,403.62 for proper disposition.

14.7      We recommended and management agreed to instruct the Municipal Accountant and the Accounting staff in charge of Pag-IBIG remittances to observe full remittance of fund contributions and loan payments deducted from employees’ compensation to avoid imposition of penalties and surcharges for late and incomplete remittances.

15.7     We recommended and management agreed to instruct the Municipal Accountant to exert more effort to trace the previous year’s balance of ₱70,906.25 and cause its remittance together with the amount of ₱308.52 due in September 2019 to avoid the accumulation of discrepancies.  Further, review postings to the respective subsidiary ledgers (SLs) and reconcile/adjust the differences immediately.

16.9     We recommended and management agreed to instruct the Municipal Accountant and the Accounting staff in  charge of BIR remittances to (a) tally the subsidiary ledgers (SL) balances with the General Ledger (GL) controlling accounts for Due to BIR before remittances are made so that all the taxes withheld during the period will be remitted in full; and (b) adjust/correct records for any discrepancy (under/over remittances) immediately on the month succeeding the remittance to avoid accumulation of erroneous balances.

17.6  We recommended and management agreed to: (a) require the persons responsible/liable to settle immediately the audit suspensions, disallowances and charges pursuant to COA Circular No. 2009-006 dated September 15, 2009; and (b) instruct the Municipal Treasurer to enforce the sanctions for continuous inaction on the NFDs/COEs, such as withholding of salaries and other emoluments due to the persons concerned.

We recommended and   management agreed to instruct the         Municipal Accountant to draw the adjusting journal entries to   correct the          misstatement of the  Property, Plant and  Equipment and        Government Equity    accounts.

We recommended that management advice the Municipal Accountant to (a) make representations with the Provincial Government of Zambales (PGZ) to secure a report of collected RPT and Taxes on Sand, Gravel and Other Quarry Products every year-end as basis of recognizing the Receivables and Income accruing to the Municipality; (b) ensure that the set-up of RPT/SET Receivables and Deferred Charges are duly adjusted upon receipt/remittance of Municipal share from the PGZ; (c) issue demand letters to concerned officials for the immediate liquidation of fund transfers to leagues to facilitate recording of funds already expended during the accounting period; (d) analyze the accounts that are dormant and long outstanding and take the necessary actions for their proper disposition in accordance with COA Circular No. 2016-005; and (e) recognize in the books of accounts the adjusting and correcting entries.
observations.reason: Similar to   the previous year, the reports of collections and transfers made by the Provincial Government of Zambales were not secured. The Accounting Unit still failed to set-up the RPT/SET Receivables and Deferred Charges for CY 2019. Outstanding fund transfers to the leagues of ₱700,000.00 were already liquidated. This finding is reiterated in Observation No. 1 of the CY 2019 AAR.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management require the Municipal Accountant to (a) conduct a thorough review and analysis of all outstanding Accounts Payable and revert to the General Fund all dormant and long-outstanding payables/balances of accounts, aged two or more, if warranted; (b) prepare JEVs to derecognize in the books all previously paid payables and adjust the entries of all the misclassified transactions and (c) review thoroughly the recorded Accounts Payable to determine whether the claims are supported by sufficient evidences and complete documentation and revert to the Unappropriated Surplus all undocumented payables; and (d) ensure that no obligation is booked up unless the same is founded on a valid claim that is supported by sufficient evidence and documentation.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant reviewed all long outstanding Accounts Payable.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management undertake the following courses of actions: advise the Municipal Treasurer to (i) conduct complete and detailed physical inventory of all properties owned by the Municipality and report the results thereof using the Report on Physical Count on Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE); and (ii) maintain Property Cards for each type of properties in coordination with the Office of the Municipal Accountant as basis for reconciliation of PPE accounts; and instruct the Municipal Accountant to (i) maintain Property, Plant and Equipment Ledger Cards (PPELC) for each type of properties and Local Road Network Ledger Card (LRNLC) for the local road networks to facilitate tracing of balances and transactions as well as reconciliation with the GL controlling accounts; (ii) exert more efforts to determine the details and information to substantiate recorded properties and provide depreciation thereof; and (iii) check and reconcile the balance of the Buildings account to reflect the correct amount in the books.
observations.managementAction: PPELCs for PPEs under SEF and Trust Fund were prepared.
observations.reason: Adjustments in Buildings account were not yet made. This finding is reiterated in Observation No. 3 of the CY 2019 AAR.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and management agreed to undertake the following courses of actions: Instruct the Municipal Treasurer to (i) facilitate the execution and issuance of contracts to the 161 tenants and ensure that lease contracts are duly accomplished as to significant details; (ii) pursue effective strategies that could intensify the collection of all unpaid rentals, surcharges and utilities; (iii) send demand letters and exert effort to locate the whereabouts of delinquent stallholders/occupants that have already ceased operations to enforce collection of prior years’ receivables; (iv) strictly implement the provisions of the New Market Code of 2004 specifically on the application of new rental rates and cancellation of the contract of lease in case of default in payment; and (v) exercise all civil remedies set forth in the Local Government Code of 1991; and Direct the Municipal Accountant to (i) maintain Special Accounts in the General Fund for the Municipality’s economic enterprises; (ii) prepare individual financial statements for the special accounts to ascertain their viability and profitability; and (iii) determine and recognize in the books of accounts all unpaid receivables arising from market rentals, surcharges and electric bills.
observations.managementAction: Notices of delinquency and demand letters were sent to stallholders. In addition, frequent follow ups were made by the Municipal Treasury Office (MTO).
observations.reason: The Contracts of Lease of some tenants were not yet executed and issued. Unpaid rentals and surcharges increased to ₱1,290,729.22 as of year-end. The MTO still failed to locate the whereabouts of some stallholders particularly those who have already ceased operations. The provisions of the New Market Code of 2004 remained unimplemented. Special Accounts for economic enterprises were not yet maintained due to lack of Accounting personnel. No Financial Statements for economic enterprises were prepared. Additional receivables from stallholders totaling to ₱686,051.22 were not recognized during the year.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and the Head of the Procuring Entity (HoPE) agreed to: ensure that all procurements are in accordance with the approved Annual Procurement Plan and Annual Investment Plan; instruct the Bids and Awards Committee, together with its TWG and Secretariat to (i) scrutinize thoroughly the documents submitted by the bidders to ensure their authenticity, veracity, and completeness as basis for the evaluation of the most advantageous and reasonable bids; (ii) conduct efficiently the post-qualification of bidders particularly in determining the sufficiency of contractors’ manpower on all awarded projects; and (iii) submit copies of the contracts and all supporting documents within five working days from execution to the Audit Team; advise the Municipal Engineer to require the issuance of Certificate of Final Acceptance to the contractor one year from project completion in compliance with Section 62.2.2 of R.A. No. 9184; and direct the Municipal Accountant to coordinate with the BAC for the completion and submission of the documentary requirements noted in audit and ensure that only disbursement vouchers (DVs) with complete documentation are processed and presented for payment.

We recommended and management agreed to instruct the: Local School Board to (i) formulate the required School Improvement Plan and Division Education Development Plan that bear expenditure items eligible for charging to the SEF Budget; (ii) stop the practice of spending SEF on non-priority expenses and ensure that only authorized expenditures are disbursed therefrom; and (iii) maximize the utilization of SEF and implement PPAs in accordance with the approved budget and SIP/DEDP; otherwise, consider realignment or reprogramming with appropriate resolutions from the Board; and Municipal Accountant to ensure that disbursements are properly supported with complete documentation and approval of authorized officials.
observations.reason: The LSB failed to formulate School Improvement Plan and Division Education Plan for CY 2019. Non-priority expenses chargeable to the fund were minimized. The utilization of the Fund registered only 53.27% for the implementation which was lower than the previous year of 63.34%.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management (a) advise the Local Development Council and the OIC-MPDC to formulate a development plan that will effectively utilize the 20% economic development fund pursuant to the main objective of DILG-DBM Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2017-1; and (b) prepare an Annual Investment Plan (AIP) that would ensure the use of the 20% of its IRA exclusively for projects that are investment and capital in nature.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality registered a completion rate of 78.79% of its programs, projects and activities funded under the 20% Development Fund.
observations.reason: Seven development projects amounting to ₱8,941,906.92 were not completed. Five PPAs charged under the Fund did not partake the nature of investment and capital expenditures. This finding is reiterated in Observation No. 8 of the CY 2019 AAR.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and management agreed to instruct the: Local Disaster Risk Reduction Management Officer (LDRRMO) to plan properly the PPAs for inclusion in the 5% LDRRMFIP, including those funded out of the current and previous years’ appropriations, and categorize the same into four thematic areas as identified under NDRRMC-DBM-DILG JMC No. 2013-1; Municipal Budget Officer and LDRRMC to prepare and submit the required registries and monthly reports for purposes of monitoring and timely audit of transactions; Municipal Engineer and the BAC to abide strictly with the schedule or project implementation period as reflected in the AIP to utilize optimally the funds allocated for the year and to avoid delay in the project execution or non-implementation of projects; and Municipal Accountant to supervise closely the submission of monthly accounts to the Office of the Auditor. For habitual late/non-submission, enforce withholding of salaries of any official/employee responsible thereof; and ensure that disbursement vouchers (DVs) are supported with complete and proper documentation before payments are made pursuant to COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive and members of the SWMB agreed to: involve closely the Barangay officials in the strict implementation on the existing environmental law and intensify information campaign towards the "no segregation, no collection" policy among the residents; provide and equip the waste segregators and collectors with personal protective equipment to protect them from hazards of handling wastes; exercise strong political will to implement the environmental laws and comply with the proper segregation, collection, and disposal of wastes; and implement the SWM fees to control the activities of business establishments as far as their specific responsibility on Solid Waste Management is concerned; and support SWM budget with a work and financial plan or list of PPAs to better evaluate the performance of the LGU in accordance with the requirements of law.
observations.managementAction: The LGU has strengthened the "No Segregation, No Collection" policy through distribution of flyers, conduct of meeting with School Heads and Solid Waste Management Coordinators and issuances of citation tickets for violators. In October 2019, the Municipality procured and distributed Personal Protective Equipment for MRF Segregators.
observations.reason: Mixed or unsegregated wastes which came from the public market were still found in the Materials Recovery Facility.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Human Resource Management Officer (HRMO) to hire casual/contract of service/job order personnel based on necessity who should be identified/attributed to specific local projects authorized by the Sanggunian for a period not exceeding six months.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive, being the Chairman of the GAD Focal Point System (GFPS) (i) ensure that the GFPS is functioning as intended to facilitate gender mainstreaming in the formulation of LGU policies, plans and programs; (ii) instruct the GFPS to gather GAD information such as gender statistics and sex-disaggregated data to serve as inputs for planning, budgeting, programming and policy formulation; (iii) strive for the full implementation of GAD-related PPAs so that the desired outcomes are attained and benefits are enjoyed by the intended beneficiaries; and (iv) evaluate and revise the submitted GAD AR to provide a reliable information on actual activities undertaken.
observations.reason: Revised GAD AR was not submitted. This finding is reiterated in Observation No. 11 of the CY 2019 AAR.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and management agreed to a) require newly hired employees, through the Human Resource Management Officer, to secure their Membership Identification (MID) Number as early as possible in order to timely remit their contributions in full; and b) instruct the Municipal Accountant to i) observe full remittance of fund contributions and loan repayments deducted from employees’ compensation including the prior year’s unremitted balances to avoid incurrence of penalty and interest on non-remittance thereof pursuant to Republic Act No. 9679; and ii) exert effort to review and identify all erroneous postings committed in the previous years to reflect the correct balance of the Due to Pag-IBIG account.
observations.reason: There were instances in which full remittances were not observed that resulted to over/under remittances. However, the same were corrected in the succeeding remittance period. There were few instances of erroneous postings to the subsidiary ledgers of the contributions and loans. Previous years’ balances were not yet traced.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to adhere faithfully to Revenue Memorandum Circular (RMC) No. 23-2012 on the prompt remittance of taxes to avoid incurrence of interests and surcharges.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant agreed to adhere faithfully to RMC No. 23-2012.
observations.reason: Prompt remittances were observed by the officials concerned; however, there were instances when taxes deducted were not remitted in exact amounts which resulted to under/over remittances.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require all persons responsible and liable to settle immediately the outstanding audit suspensions, disallowances and charges.
observations.reason: The payment of the disallowances is ongoing and done thru payroll deduction.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant exert more diligence in analyzing the prior years’ balances of ₱249,403.62 and conduct immediate reconciliation with the GSIS to determine the actual and correct balance of the Due to GSIS account.
observations.reason:
The Accounting staff still failed to conduct immediate reconciliation with the GSIS; thus, the amount of ₱249,403.62 remained undocumented and unsettled.
Reiterated under Observation No. 16 of the CY 2018 AAR.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and management agreed that: refrain from granting cash advances for types of expenses not falling within the scope and definition of cash advances as provided under COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997 and discontinue the practice of procuring goods and repairing vehicles thru cash advances; and instruct the Municipal Accountant to (i) require the concerned personnel to liquidate their cash advances within the reglementary period; (ii) stop the practice of granting new cash advances without the liquidation of previous ones; and (iii) ensure that all grants and liquidations of cash advances are supported with complete documentations.
observations.reason:
Similar to the previous year, lapses were prevalent in the handling of cash advances; hence, disbursements not falling within the scope and definition of cash advances still existed. Cash advances were still granted to AOs despite previous unliquidated balances. Further, liquidations were still made beyond the reglementary period and with incomplete documentation.
Reiterated in Observation No. 5 of the CY 2018 AAR.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and the management agreed to: ensure that all procurements are in accordance with the approved Annual Procurement Plan and Annual Investment Plan; instruct the Bids and Awards Committee to comply with posting, conduct post-qualification, ensure completeness of documents, provide contracts within 5 working days to the Audit Team; and advise Municipal Engineer to require issuance of Certificate of Final Acceptance one year from project completion.
observations.reason:
Four projects were implemented despite non-inclusion in the APP and AIP.
There has been a continuous practice to award contracts to construction companies that had simultaneous project duration and/or overlapping work schedule. Contracts and disbursement vouchers submitted to the Audit Team still lacked complete documentation.
No written justification/explanation on the latent violations of R.A. No. 9184 was submitted to the Audit Team with regards to the aforementioned infractions.
Reiterated in Observation No. 7 of the CY 2018 AAR.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management instruct the: Municipal Planning Development Coordinator (MPDC) to properly plan the PPAs for inclusion in the 20% Development Plan and refrain from charging expenditures that do not partake the nature of capital investments or those not essential or relevant to the attainment of desirable socio-economic development and environmental management outcomes as identified under DILG-DBM JMC No. 2017 - 1; MPDC, together with the Municipal Engineer, to abide strictly on the schedule or project implementation period as reflected in the AIP to optimally utilize the fund allocated for the year and to avoid delay in the project execution; and Municipal Budget Officer (MBO) to maintain a separate registry for the utilization of both current and continuing appropriations of PPAs funded under the 20% Development Fund for purposes of proper monitoring.
observations.reason:
Similar to the previous year, management included projects which were not supportive of the purpose for which the fund was intended.
MPDC and other offices were able to improve project implementation for CY 2018 but still included projects not considered eligible for charging against the 20% Development Fund.
MBO was able to maintain a separate record; however, the same was not regularly updated.
Reiterated in Observation No. 9 of the CY 2018 AAR.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management adopt more effective strategies to collect tax arrears; otherwise, exercise all available remedies to ensure the realization of revenues.
observations.reason:
The LGU failed to adopt stricter collection strategies and resort to civil remedies for the collection of fees and related surcharges as evidenced by uncollected income from delinquent stallholders in the public market and commercial spaces/lot for lease as of December 31, 2018 totaling to ₱2,241,476.94.
Reiterated in Observation No. 6 of the CY 2018 AAR.

We recommended that management require the Municipal Accountant to (a) conduct a thorough review and analysis of all outstanding Accounts Payable and revert to the General Fund all dormant and long-outstanding payables/balances of accounts, aged two or more, if warranted; (b) prepare JEVs to derecognize in the books all previously paid payables and adjust the entries of all the misclassified transactions as follows:
To derecognize previously paid liabilities
To correct misclassification of liabilities
review thoroughly the recorded Accounts Payable to determine whether the claims are valid and duly supported with complete documentation and revert to the Unappropriated Surplus all undocumented payables; and (d) ensure that no obligation is booked up unless the same is founded on a valid claim that is supported by sufficient evidence and documentation.

We recommended that management undertake the following courses of actions:
advise the Municipal Treasury Office to (i) conduct complete and detailed physical inventory of all properties owned by the Municipality and report the results thereof using the Report on Physical Count on Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE); and (ii) maintain Property Cards for each type of properties in coordination with the Office of the Municipal Accountant as basis for reconciliation of PPE accounts; and
instruct the Municipal Accountant to (i) maintain Property, Plant and Equipment Ledger Cards for each type of properties; (ii) exert more efforts to determine the details and information to substantiate recorded properties and provide depreciation thereof; and (iii) check and reconcile the balance of Buildings account to reflect the correct amount in the books.

We recommended that management direct the:
Municipal Accountant to (i) refrain from granting cash advances to accountable officers in amounts beyond their approved maximum cash accountability and those AOs with expired fidelity bonds so as to protect the interest of the Municipality from possible losses in case the former failed to render account for such cash advances or had abandoned their post; (ii) stop the practice of granting new cash advances without the liquidation of previous ones; and (iii) require the concerned personnel to liquidate their cash advances within the reglementary period provided by law; and
employee in charge of the Confidential Fund to make representation with the Intelligence and Confidential Fund Audit Unit (ICFAU) of COA Central Office for the prompt issuance of credit notice on prior years’ unliquidated cash advances which shall be the basis for recording thereafter.

We recommended and management agreed to undertake the following courses of actions:
Instruct the Municipal Treasurer to (i) facilitate the execution and issuance of contracts to the 161 tenants and ensure that lease contracts are duly accomplished as to significant details; (ii) pursue effective strategies that could intensify the collection of all unpaid rentals, surcharges and utilities; (iii) send demand letters and exert effort to locate the whereabouts of delinquent stallholders/occupants that have already ceased operations to enforce collection of prior years’ receivables; (iv) strictly implement the provisions of the New Market Code of 2004 specifically on the application of new rental rates and cancellation of the contract of lease in case of default in payment; and (v) exercise all civil remedies set forth in the Local Government Code of 1991; and
Direct the Municipal Accountant to (i) maintain Special Accounts in the General Fund for the Municipality’s economic enterprises; (ii) prepare individual financial statements for the special accounts to ascertain their viability and profitability; and (iii) determine and recognize in the books of accounts all unpaid receivables arising from market rentals, surcharges and electric bills.

We recommended and management agreed to instruct the:
Local School Board to (i) formulate the required School Improvement Plan and Division Education Development Plan that bear expenditure items eligible for charging to the SEF Budget; (ii) stop the practice of spending SEF on non-priority expenses and ensure that only authorized expenditures are disbursed therefrom; and (iii) maximize the utilization of SEF and implement PPAs in accordance with the approved budget and SIP/DEDP; otherwise, consider realignment or reprogramming with appropriate resolutions from the Board; and
Municipal Accountant to ensure that disbursements are properly supported with complete documentation and approval of authorized officials.

We recommended that Management (a) advise the Local Development Council and the OIC-MPDC to formulate a development plan that will effectively utilize the 20% economic development fund pursuant to the main objective of DILG-DBM Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2017-1; and (b) prepare an Annual Investment Plan (AIP) that would ensure the use of the 20% of its IRA exclusively for projects that are investment and capital in nature.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the:
Municipal Accountant to (i) coordinate with the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) for the completion and immediate submission of the documentary deficiencies noted in audit; (ii) ensure that disbursement vouchers (DVs) are duly supported with complete and proper documentation before payments are made; and (iii) supervise closely the submission of monthly accounts, including copies of perfected contracts, to the Office of the Auditor; and
Municipal Engineer to (i) include all necessary information or specifications of the projects, such as length of the road, in the Plans/Program of Works (POW); (ii) ensure the conformance of the implemented PPAs with the approved Plans/POW; and (iii) oversee carefully the projects in all phases of implementation/s to satisfy the needs of the public within the bounds of laws, rules and regulations.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Human Resource Management Officer (HRMO) to hire casual/contract of service/job order personnel based on necessity who should be identified/attributed to specific local projects authorized by the Sanggunian for a period not exceeding six months.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive, being the Chairman of the GAD Focal Point System (GFPS) (i) ensure that the GFPS is functioning as intended to facilitate gender mainstreaming in the formulation of LGU policies, plans and programs; (ii) instruct the GFPS to gather GAD information such as gender statistics and sex-disaggregated data to serve as inputs for planning, budgeting, programming and policy formulation for the setting up and maintenance of GAD database; (iii) strive for the full implementation of GAD-related PPAs so that the desired outcomes are attained and benefits are enjoyed by the intended beneficiaries; and (iv) evaluate and revise the submitted GAD AR to provide a reliable information on actual activities undertaken.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Accountant:
exert diligent effort to trace the source of undocumented and unremitted balance of ₱249,403.62 by comparing the alpha list of employees with processed payrolls and statement of remittances;
adhere faithfully to R.A. No. 8291 to avoid incurrence of penalty and interest on delayed or non-remittance of amounts due to GSIS; and
instruct the Accounting staff to (i) analyze the causes of discrepancy between the amounts withheld and the remittances; (ii) deduct from the salaries of employees concerned any deficiencies in the premiums withheld and refund thru the voucher system the excess deductions in the payroll; (iii) for excess remittances to the GSIS, send a request for possible offsetting in the succeeding month’s remittances provided the individual employees affected have been identified; and (iv) see to it that all necessary adjustments are taken up in the books thru issuance of a JEV.

We recommended and management agreed to a) require newly hired employees, through the Human Resource Management Officer, to secure their Membership Identification (MID) Number as early as possible in order to timely remit their contributions in full; and b) instruct the Municipal Accountant to i) observe full remittance of fund contributions and loan repayments deducted from employees’ compensation including the prior year’s unremitted balances to avoid incurrence of penalty and interest on non-remittance thereof pursuant to Republic Act No. 9679, and ii) exert effort to review and identify all erroneous postings committed in the previous years to reflect the correct balance of the Due to Pag-IBIG account.

We recommended and the management agreed to instruct the (a) Municipal Accountant to exert effort in tracing/analyzing the erroneous postings in the previous years to completely reconcile the ledger balance with the schedule; and (b) Human Resource Management Office to set a deadline for the newly hired personnel to secure their personal identification/registration numbers with PhilHealth to avoid unremitted premiums.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to adhere faithfully to Revenue Memorandum Circular (RMC) No. 23-2012 on the prompt remittance of taxes to avoid incurrence of interests and surcharges.
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require all persons responsible and liable to settle immediately the outstanding audit suspensions, disallowances and charges.

We recommended that the:
Local Chief Executive (LCE) reconstitute an Inventory/Appraisal/Disposal Committee to conduct a complete annual physical inventory in order to (i) identify unserviceable or fully-depreciated properties for disposal; (ii) provide appraisal value for properties needed to be recorded in the books in the absence of any valid acquisition documents as basis for costing; and (iii) make available all necessary reports that perennially hindered the discovery of any discrepancies/errors between the records kept by the Accounting Office and the MTO; and

Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer, in coordination with the Inventory/Appraisal/Disposal Committee: (i) maintain the required documents such as PPELCs and Property Cards; (ii) exert more effort to secure documents such as deeds of donation from the donors of donated properties, proofs of ownership for untitled lands, and the like to warrant the existence and validity of the recorded PPE accounts; and (iii) make a periodic reconciliation of records to immediately detect errors/irregularities.
observations.managementAction:
The Local Chief Executive issued EO No. 67, s. 2018 dated 06/04/18 re-organizing the composition of the Disposal Committee of the Municipality.
The Municipal Treasury Office submitted a partial inventory report containing informations gathered from the Accounting Office.
observations.reason:
No Inventory/Appraisal/Disposal Committee was reconstituted to conduct a complete annual physical inventory of all PPEs owned by the LGU.
Only motor vehicles and heavy equipment subject for disposal were identified by the Committee.
The same lacked important pertinent information which hindered reconciliation of the recorded balance per books as against the submitted inventory report.
The Accounting Office failed to maintain PPELCs for all properties recorded under the Special Education Fund and Trust Fund books. Likewise, no PPELCs were maintained to support several properties purchased under the General Fund totaling to ₱25,756,822.06.
Comparatively, the Municipal Treasury Office failed to implement the prior year’s recommendation to maintain the corresponding Property Cards (PC) for each PPE item to easily determine the needed data for every PPE owned by the LGU.
No reconciliation of records was made to errors or irregularities.
Reiterated under Observation No. 4 of the CY 2018 AAR.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the management instruct the:
Municipal Budget Officer (MBO) to properly obligate payments of programs/projects based on fulfilled contracts or delivered services for accuracy of recording and reporting; and
Municipal Accountant to draw the necessary adjusting entries to recognize three completed projects under the Continuing Appropriations in the total amount of ₱2,526,502.09.
observations.managementAction:
Due to the prior year’s observation, the MBO is now obligating transactions based on fulfilled contracts or delivered services.
The Municipal Accountant recognized in the books of accounts the required correcting entries in September 2018.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management (a) require concerned members of the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) to refund the overpayment of honoraria in the total amount of ₱262,558.25; and (b) instruct the Municipal Accountant to check carefully subsequent claims if BAC is eligible to collect such amount of honoraria pursuant to Section 5 of DBM Circular No. 2004-5A dated October 7, 2005.
observations.managementAction: During CY 2017, the BAC refunded a total amount of ₱70,907.00 thru deductions from CY 2018 honoraria.
observations.reason:
The remaining balance of ₱140,251.50 remained unrefunded.
The Municipal Accountant still failed to monitor carefully the limitation of the grant of honoraria to the BAC; thus, honoraria received during CY 2018 by one BAC member and three members of the BAC Secretariat were again excessive totaling to ₱90,357.00.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management: stop the practice of granting cash advances to any personnel of the Department of Education, instead assign a permanent employee either duly appointed or designated disbursing officer to perform the disbursing functions for the Special Education Fund; and advise the Local School Board to (i) stop charging prior year’s transactions to current year’s appropriations to avoid issuance of Notice of Disallowance; and (ii) maximize the utilization of the fund to optimally achieve its very purpose of financing the priority education projects of the LGU.
observations.managementAction: All cash advances were granted to duly designated disbursing officers.
observations.reason:
No prior years’ transactions were charged to current appropriation.
Only 63.11% utilization rate was registered during the year which was higher than the accomplishment rate of 37% only in CY 2017; however, still only partially implemented as several PPAs remain non-implemented.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management: instruct the Budget Officer to (i) appropriate and set aside not less than 5% of the estimated revenue from regular sources for LDRRMF; and (ii) maintain the required separate Registry of Appropriations, Allotments and Obligations (RAAO) for QRF, MOOE and CO to facilitate the determination of the unexpended balance of the funds; and advise the LDRRMO to (i) include in the LDRRMFIP the programs, projects and activities to be undertaken out of the unexpended balance of previous years; (ii) determine calamity-related PPAs and categorize them as to the four thematic areas pursuant to DILG Policy; and (iii) submit report on utilization of DRRMF to the Office of the Auditor on or before the 15th day after the end of each month.
observations.managementAction:
During the current year, appropriations for LDRRMF summed up to ₱9,446,413.75 or 5.08%.
The MSWMB conducted a meeting and other actions.
observations.reason:
The Municipal Budget Officer still failed to maintain a separate registry showing the utilization of the current and continuing appropriations for PPAs financed thru the 5% LDRRMF for purposes of monitoring. Utilization Report was only submitted at year-end contrary to Section 5.1.5 of COA Circular 2012-002.
The amount of ₱12,711,614.72 pertaining to the CY 2017 balance of the Trust Liabilities – DRRM Fund account representing the unexpended balances of the Fund for the years 2014 to 2017 was not incorporated in the LDRRMFIP.
PPAs included in the LDRRMFIP were still not categorized into the four thematic areas. Utilization Report was only submitted at year-end and not monthly.
Reiterated in Observation No. 10 of the CY 2018 AAR.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive and members of the SWMB: exert more effort to coordinate with the EMB and expedite the establishment of the Sanitary Landfill; ensure the proper rehabilitation and operations of the waste disposal facility and Residual Containment Area; provide and equip the waste segregators and collectors with personal protective equipment to protect them from hazards of handling wastes; intensify the information campaign on the “no segregation, no collection policy” among the residents; prioritize the implementation of programs identified in the SWMP and other environmental programs; and rationalize all procurement activities by complying strictly with the required documentation processes under R.A. No. 9184.
observations.managementAction: The MSWMB conducted a meeting with all the Punong Barangays to disseminate the information. "No Segregation, No Collection" tarpaulins were placed in public areas, especially in the marketplace. Flyers were also distributed informing the public of the strict implementation of the policy effective November 8, 2018.
observations.reason:
No Sanitary Landfill was established in CY 2018.
Unsegregated wastes were still found in the Residual Containment Area. Waste collectors and segregators as well as pickers were not equipped with personal protective equipment such as masks, gloves and boots to protect them from hazards of handling wastes and health risks.
Although a Municipal Ordinance provided for SWM fees and an appropriation was made for SWMPF, no work and financial plan or list of PPAs were identified to properly utilize the Fund.
Reiterated in Observation No. 12 of the CY 2018 AAR.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the MSWD Office (a) ensure that the SEA-K fund assistance be granted only to legitimate members of SKAs/SKGs with priority given to Pantawid Pamilya beneficiaries and see to it that the same were utilized solely for duly approved and intended purposes; (b) encourage SKAs/SKGs officers to exert earnest effort in collecting repayments of the amount granted to the beneficiaries; otherwise, impose the necessary sanctions thereto pursuant to the Saligang Batas; and (c) regularly monitor the implementation of the Sustainable Livelihood Program of the Department of Social Welfare and Development to achieve the noble objective of providing an aggressive credit and savings mobilization to low-income members of the community, thereby promoting the growth of micro-enterprises.
observations.managementAction:
